findings.title: ConfirmDialog busy-guard resets unconditionally on reopen, not on prior confirm() settlement
evidence: ConfirmDialog.vue onOpen() (fired by watch(() => props.open) on every false→true transition) unconditionally sets busy.value = false and errorMessage.value = ''.

onConfirmClick() is the only place busy is set true, and it is only cleared in its own finally block after props.confirm() settles — there is no coupling between 'a confirm() call is still in flight' and 'the dialog is about to reopen'.

If a future/other caller flips `open` false→true externally (e.g. derived from unrelated reactive state, a route change, or a hotkey) while a prior confirm() promise from the same component instance is still pending, onOpen silently re-arms the confirm button (busy=false) before the first destructive call has resolved.

A user could then click confirm again, firing props.confirm() a second time concurrently with the first (e.g. doRepublish/doRevoke invoked twice against the same resource) — a double-fire of a destructive/security action that the busy-guard is specifically designed to prevent.

Not exploitable through the two current call sites (KeepAlivePage's `open` is derived from a state machine where 'confirming' is unreachable again until 'publishing'/'idle' has fully unwound, and BloggerCard's backdrop blocks the only button that could reopen it) — this is a latent contract gap in the shared component, not a live break in this diff.
findings.recommendation: Track an in-flight confirm() call across open/close transitions (e.g. a `pendingConfirm` promise ref) and have onOpen refuse to reset busy while one exists, or block reopening entirely until it settles.

findings.title: BloggerCard's doRevoke never rejects, silently defeating ConfirmDialog's documented fail-open contract for an OAuth revocation
evidence: ConfirmDialog.vue's contract (stated in its own header comment) is: confirm-callback reject → dialog stays open, shows inline error, button re-enabled; resolve → dialog closes as a success signal.
BloggerCard.vue's doRevoke() catches all errors internally and routes them to toastError(e) instead of rethrowing, with an explicit comment stating this is intentional so doRevoke 'never rejects'.

Because doRevoke never rejects, ConfirmDialog's onConfirmClick always takes the resolve path (`await props.confirm()` succeeds) and always emits update:open(false), closing the modal — even when revokeBlogger() actually failed on the backend.

For a security-adjacent action (OAuth token revocation), the modal closing is a plausible signal to the user that revocation succeeded; if the toast is missed (auto-dismissed, scrolled past, or suppressed by a notification-fatigue habit) the user has no other closing-the-loop indicator that the revoke failed.

This matches pre-existing window.confirm() UX (intentional preservation per the diff's own comment) so it is not a regression in isolation, but it is now built on top of a component whose stated purpose is exactly to surface confirm failures inline — the one shipped real-world usage of the reusable component silently opts out of that guarantee for a destructive/security flow.
findings.recommendation: For OAuth-revoke specifically, let doRevoke rethrow the error instead of swallowing it, so ConfirmDialog's inline-error path (which is more visible/durable than a toast) surfaces revoke failures, matching the component's own documented contract.

residual_risks: webui_app/templates/base.html vendors bootstrap-icons via a package whose CSS banner comment says 'v1.10.5' while the plan doc and code comment assert the actual glyph content is v1.11.0 (upstream mislabeled banner, said to have been manually verified). This review did not independently diff the glyph/codepoint table against 1.11.0; if that verification was wrong, any legacy Jinja template using an icon name added between 1.10.5 and 1.11.0 would silently render a missing/blank glyph with no visible error (icon fonts fail silently, unlike Icon.vue's console.warn path). Current usage sample (grep across webui_app templates) only showed icons that predate 1.10.5, so no observed breakage, but this is an unverified assumption baked into an offline-availability guarantee.

Icon.vue renders nothing (silent, only a console.warn) for any name not present in its hardcoded ICONS map. Currently exercised names (box-arrow-up-right, shield-check, exclamation-triangle-fill) are all present, but as more legacy bi-* usages migrate to Icon.vue under W7's plan, a copy/paste of an icon name not yet added to ICONS will disappear without any visible UI signal — only discoverable via devtools console.

The no-cdn-icon-usage.spec.ts guard only regex-scans literal `class="...bi bi-*..."` substrings inside .vue files; it would not catch a `bi bi-*` class string assembled dynamically in a computed property or composable and interpolated via `:class`, so the anti-regression guarantee is narrower than its stated intent.
